input.explanation: Revert booleanOptions and FormAutocomplete to clean original shape since the real fix is upstream in fieldValuesToExpressions.

messages.body:
The root cause was in `fieldValuesToExpressions`. Boolean values like `"Yes"` were falling through to `WrapWithInvisibleDelimiter("Yes")`, storing `"\u200BYes\u200B"` (with invisible zero-width space chars). When the form re-initialized, `FormAutocomplete` tried to match `"\u200BYes\u200B"` against `option.value === "Yes"` — no match, so the field appeared blank.

The fix adds an `isBoolean` check that bypasses `WrapWithInvisibleDelimiter` for boolean fields, keeping the value as plain `"Yes"`/`"No"` — consistent with how `getLogicalExpressionFunc` already handles the boolean case.

messages.body: Found the issue. In the tokenizer's `cases` block, the order matters — Monarch evaluates cases top-to-bottom and stops at the first match. `@booleanValues` is listed **last**, after `@keywords`. Since `"Yes"` and `"No"` are not in the `keywords` array they'd fall through, but `"NO"` actually **is** in the keywords list (`"NO"` is a SQL keyword). More critically, `"Yes"` and `"No"` would match `@keywords` check first if they were there, but the real problem is that `@booleanValues` must come **before** `@keywords` and `@operators` to take priority.

diffs.oldStr:
cases: {
            "@operators": "operator",
            "@logicalOperators": "logicalOperator",
            "@builtinVariables": "predefined",
            "@builtinFunctions": "predefined",
            "@keywords": "keyword",
            "@default": "identifier",
            "@booleanValues": "booleanValues",
          },
diffs.newStr:
cases: {
            "@booleanValues": "booleanValues",
            "@operators": "operator",
            "@logicalOperators": "logicalOperator",
            "@builtinVariables": "predefined",
            "@builtinFunctions": "predefined",
            "@keywords": "keyword",
            "@default": "identifier",
          },

input.explanation: Move @booleanValues to the top of the cases block so it is evaluated before @keywords, giving it priority in the Monarch tokenizer.

messages.body: The Monarch tokenizer evaluates `cases` in order and stops at the first match. `@booleanValues` was listed last, so it was never reached — the `@default` case (`"identifier"`) matched first for `"Yes"`/`"No"`. Moving `@booleanValues` to the top ensures it takes priority over all other cases.

messages.body:
The issue is in the "Paste All" handler. Unlike "Paste Above" and "Paste Below", it calls `setState` directly with a value (not a callback), so it doesn't account for the existing state. But more importantly, the `logical_operator` logic is incomplete:

```tsx
i === 0 && e.logical_operator === "NOT"
  ? e.logical_operator
  : ""
```

This only preserves `"NOT"` at index 0, but **clears all other valid operators** (like `"AND"`, `"OR"`) for every row including non-zero rows. Compare with "Paste Below" which correctly falls through to `e.logical_operator` when the condition isn't met.

The fix — mirror the logic from "Paste Above/Below":

```tsx
setState(
  copied.map((e, i) => ({
    ...e,
    line_number: i,
    logical_operator:
      i === 0 && e.logical_operator !== "NOT"
        ? ""
        : i !== 0 && e.logical_operator === "NOT"
        ? ""
        : e.logical_operator,
  }))
);
```

Or more concisely:

```tsx
logical_operator:
  (i === 0 && e.logical_operator === "NOT") || i !== 0
    ? e.logical_operator === "NOT" && i !== 0
      ? ""
      : e.logical_operator
    : "",
```

The cleanest version matching the pattern used in "Paste Above":

```tsx
logical_operator:
  (i === 0 && e.logical_operator === "NOT") ||
  (i !== 0 && e.logical_operator !== "NOT")
    ? e.logical_operator
    : "",
```

This ensures:
- Index 0: only `"NOT"` is cleared (any other operator is preserved... wait, actually index 0 should have no prefix operator)
- Index > 0: `"NOT"` is cleared, all others (`"AND"`, `"OR"`) are preserved
I want if i = 0 then logical_operator should be either NOT or empty string

The current logic already does that — when `i === 0` and `logical_operator === "NOT"` it keeps `"NOT"`, otherwise it sets `""`. So for `i === 0`, the result is already either `"NOT"` or `""`.

The actual issue is for `i !== 0` — it always sets `""`, wiping out valid operators like `"AND"` / `"OR"`. Fix:

diffs.oldStr:
logical_operator:
                    i === 0 && e.logical_operator === "NOT"
                      ? e.logical_operator
                      : "",
diffs.newStr:
logical_operator:
                    i === 0 && e.logical_operator !== "NOT"
                      ? ""
                      : i !== 0 && e.logical_operator === "NOT"
                      ? ""
                      : e.logical_operator,

input.explanation: Fix paste-all logical_operator logic to preserve AND/OR for i !== 0, and allow only NOT or empty for i === 0.

messages.body:
Now the rules are:
- `i === 0`: keeps `"NOT"` if present, otherwise `""`
- `i !== 0`: clears `"NOT"` (invalid prefix position), keeps everything else (`"AND"`, `"OR"`)
Same for fieldValues
The `fieldValues` is derived from `copied` before applying the `logical_operator` corrections. We need to derive it from the already-corrected state instead:

input.explanation: Derive fieldValues from the corrected state so logical_operator fixes are reflected in both state and field values.

messages.body: Now `fieldValues` is derived from `newState` (the corrected expressions), so both `setValues` and `setState` are in sync with the same corrected `logical_operator` values.

Please help me fix the following errors in c:\Users\VM112\source\repos\solvas-am-biz-components\src\components\expression-builder\expression-builder.component.tsx:**src\components\expression-builder\expression-builder.component.tsx**

```
[ts] Line 618: 'await' has no effect on the type of this expression.
```
Let me read the specific area around line 618 to understand the issue.

Line 618 is inside the `handleSubmit` callback:

```tsx
if (setValue) {
  await setValue(modelExpression);  // line 618
}
```

The issue is that `setValue` is typed as a synchronous function returning `void`, so `await` has no effect on it. Looking at the prop interface:

```tsx
setValue?: (
  value: ModelExpression,
  shouldValidate?: boolean | undefined
) => void;
```

The fix is to simply remove the `await` from that call.

diffs.oldStr: await setValue(modelExpression);
diffs.newStr: setValue(modelExpression);

input.explanation: Remove await from synchronous setValue call to fix the TypeScript error.

messages.body: Done. Removed `await` from `setValue(modelExpression)` since the prop is typed as `() => void` (synchronous), making `await` a no-op that TypeScript correctly flags.
I want this setValue(modelExpression); to return before executing handleOnCloseButtonClick

The current code already does that — `setValue(modelExpression)` runs before `handleOnCloseButtonClick?.()`. But if `setValue` is actually async in practice (just typed as `void`), you'd want to `await` it properly.

There are two approaches:

1. Keep the prop type as-is and cast to force the `await` — quick but hacky
2. Update the `setValue` prop type to return `Promise<void> | void` — the correct fix

I'd recommend option 2. Here's what changes:
- Update the `setValue` prop type in `ExpressionBuilderProps` to `Promise<void> | void`
- Re-add `await` on the call site

diffs.oldStr: setValue(modelExpression);
diffs.newStr: await setValue(modelExpression);

messages.body: Now `setValue` can be either sync or async, and `await` will correctly wait for it to complete before `handleOnCloseButtonClick?.()` is called. No more TypeScript warning either.

messages.body: In this toggle how can I do something like when there is a value and toggled to V and then toggle to F,  the value should clear but when toggle back to v we need data back similar to FormValueSelector

Looking at the FormValueSelector component, you need to cache the `val` field value when toggling to `F`, clear it, and restore it when toggling back to `V`.

Here's the minimal change — add a ref to cache the value and update `handleToggleChange`:
Add a `useRef` to cache the val field value, then update `handleToggleChange` to save/restore it on toggle:

input.explanation: Add useRef import, cachedValRef, and update handleToggleChange to cache/restore val field value on toggle.

messages.body:
Here's what was changed:

- Added `useRef` import
- `cachedValRef` stores the `valFieldId` value before clearing it when toggling to `F`
- On toggle to `F`: reads current value from Formik `values`, caches it in the ref, clears the field
- On toggle back to `V`: restores the cached value via `setFieldValue`

One caveat: `values` in the `useCallback` dep array means the callback re-creates when any form value changes. If that's a concern, you can read the value directly from the field instead:

```ts
const [{ value: valFieldValue }] = useField(valFieldId);
```

Then replace `(values as Record<string, unknown>)[valFieldId]` with `valFieldValue` and swap `values` for `valFieldValue` in the deps array — this scopes the dependency to just that one field.
Let me find the expression builder file first.

The `values` here is a flat `{ [x: string]: string }` object managed by Formik's `setValues`. Since there's no `useRef` available per-row (it's inside a `.map()`), the cleanest approach is to store cached values inside the same `values` object using a hidden key like `cached_value_<index>`.

input.explanation: Cache the value_ key into cached_value_ when toggling to F, and restore it when toggling back to V.

messages.body:
That's the only change needed. Here's what it does:

- Toggle `V → F`: saves current `value_<index>` into `cached_value_<index>`, clears `value_<index>`
- Toggle `F → V`: restores `value_<index>` from `cached_value_<index>`

The `cached_value_<index>` key lives silently in the Formik values bag — it won't affect any rendering or submission logic since nothing reads it except this toggle handler.
Can't we use promise to wait for setValue to execute first and then handleOnCloseButtonClick?
Looking at the `handleSubmit` callback:

Yes — `setValue` (the prop) is synchronous (void return), but `handleOnCloseButtonClick` is called right after it. You can wrap `setValue` in a Promise to guarantee sequencing. Here's the targeted change in `handleSubmit`:

input.explanation: Wrap setValue in a Promise so handleOnCloseButtonClick is guaranteed to run after it completes.

messages.body:
That's the only change. The `new Promise<void>` wrapper ensures `setValue(modelExpression)` executes and the microtask queue flushes before `handleOnCloseButtonClick` is called.

One thing worth noting: since `setValue` is synchronous, the Promise resolves immediately in the same tick — so this guarantees ordering but won't help if `setValue` triggers async side effects (like a state update that needs a re-render). If that's the case, the real solution would be to change the `setValue` prop signature to return a `Promise<void>` and `await` it directly.

You're right. Since `setValue` is synchronous and just triggers a React state update, wrapping it in a Promise doesn't help — the state update is batched and hasn't been committed yet when `handleOnCloseButtonClick` fires.

The correct approach is to use a `useEffect` to watch for the value being set, then call `handleOnCloseButtonClick`. Let me revert the Promise change and implement that instead:

input.explanation: Add pendingClose state and useEffect to call handleOnCloseButtonClick after setValue's state update has been committed.

messages.body:
Here's how it works:

1. `setValue(modelExpression)` triggers the parent's state update
2. `setPendingClose(true)` schedules a re-render
3. After React commits both state updates, the `useEffect` fires — at this point `setValue`'s effect has already been applied — and calls `handleOnCloseButtonClick`

This guarantees the close happens after the parent has received and processed the new value.

input.explanation: Reset isEditorEnabled and isPassThrough whenever the dialog closes (open=false), unconditionally, so stale complex-rule state is cleared before the next open.

messages.body:
The change moves the reset out of the cleanup function and into the effect body, triggered when `open` is `false`. This means:

- When the dialog closes (after delete or cancel), `isPassThrough` and `isEditorEnabled` are reset immediately.
- Next time the dialog opens, it starts fresh — no stale "Complex Rule" state.
- The `isComplexRule` effect still correctly re-enables the editor when opening with a complex rule.
editorRef?.current?.getValue() also does not clean when we delete and the view editor button does not work
Let me look at the current state of the file to understand the full flow.

Two problems here:

1. `editorRef.current` holds the Monaco editor instance — its value persists because the editor is not re-mounted when the dialog reopens. It needs to be cleared when `open` becomes `false`.
2. The `toggleEditor` guard `if (isPassThroughCode(editorRef?.current?.getValue() ?? ""))` checks the stale editor value (still has the old complex rule code), so it returns early and the button does nothing.

Both stem from the same root: the editor value isn't cleared on close. Fix both by clearing the editor value and `isEditorDirty` in the same `!open` reset block:

input.explanation: Clear the Monaco editor value, dirty flag, and exprDescription when the dialog closes so the toggle button works correctly on next open.

messages.body:
Here's what was happening and what the fix does:

- `editorRef.current` is a stable ref to the Monaco editor instance — it survives dialog close/reopen since the component stays mounted. So after deleting a complex rule, the editor still held the old passthrough code.
- `toggleEditor` checks `isPassThroughCode(editorRef?.current?.getValue())` at the top and returns early if true — that's why the button appeared broken.
- `exprDescription.current` also held the stale description.

Now when `open` becomes `false`, all three are reset: `editorRef.current.setValue("")`, `isEditorDirty.current = false`, and `exprDescription.current = ""` — so the next open starts completely clean.

The real issue with point 3 is simpler — just use `index != null` (loose equality) which handles both `null` and `undefined`:

```tsx
const [, , valueHelpers] = useField<string | null>(
  index != null ? `value_${index}` : "missing-index"
);
```

This correctly handles all three cases:
- `index = 0` → `"value_0"` ✓
- `index = null` → `"missing-index"` ✓
- `index = undefined` (optional prop not passed) → `"missing-index"` ✓

messages.body: will this,  required={required ?? false}, work in FormFormulaEditor

Yes, `required={required ?? false}` will work correctly in `FormFormulaEditor`.

Since `required` is typed as `boolean | undefined` in `FormFormulaEditorProps`, the `??` (nullish coalescing) operator will:

- Pass the value through if `required` is `true` or `false`
- Fall back to `false` only if `required` is `undefined`

This is equivalent to `required={required || false}` in this case, but `??` is more precise since it only catches `null`/`undefined` (not falsy values like `0` or `""`).

That said, you could simplify it even further to just:

```tsx
required={required}
```

Since `FormTextField`'s `required` prop likely accepts `boolean | undefined`, and `undefined` would be treated as falsy/not-required anyway. The `?? false` is redundant unless `FormTextField` strictly requires a `boolean` (not `boolean | undefined`).

`FormCustomDatePicker` doesn't handle required validation internally either — it relies on Yup via `validationSchema` on Formik too. The difference is that `FormCustomDatePicker` calls `helpers.setTouched(true)` on blur, which triggers Formik to run validation and show the error.

The problem with `FormFormulaEditor` is that the `FormTextField` inside it is `readOnly` — the user never focuses/blurs it, so `touched` is never set to `true`, and Formik never shows the error even if you have a Yup schema.

The fix is to call `helpers.setTouched(true)` when the form submits without a value. The cleanest place is in `FormFormulaEditor` using `useFormikContext` to detect submission:

The issue is clear. The `FormTextField` is `readOnly`, so `touched` is never set to `true` by user interaction. The fix is to use `useFormikContext` to watch `submitCount` and call `helpers.setTouched(true)` when a submit is attempted.

```
[ts] Line 486: 'initialValues' implicitly has type 'any' because it does not have a type annotation and is referenced directly or indirectly in its own initializer.
```

Line 486 is the `useState` for `initialValues`. The error is a circular reference — `expressionToFieldValues` is defined after `ExpressionBuilder` but used inside it to initialize state. TypeScript can't infer the type because of this ordering.

The fix is to add an explicit type to the `useState` call:

input.explanation: Replace the inline type with the already-defined ExpressionBuilderFieldValues interface to resolve the circular reference type inference error.

messages.body: `ExpressionBuilderFieldValues` is already defined as `{ [x: string]: string }` in the same file, so using it here breaks the circular inference chain and resolves the error.

Looking at the bug: when you drag a date field row to a position where the field has a different data type, the `value_` for that row still holds an ISO date string (e.g. `"2026-05-06T00:00:00.000Z"`). The `FormCustomDatePicker` at the new position is gone (since the field is now numeric), but the `FormTextField` in `FormValueField` receives that ISO string as its value — and since `FormCustomDatePicker` previously set a touched+error state on that field name, the error persists.

The root cause: when reordering, the `value_` is carried over as-is, but the data type of the field at that index has changed. The stale ISO date string in a non-date field triggers the date picker's validation error from the previous render cycle.

The fix: when building `updatedFieldValues` during reorder, clear the `value_` and `value2_` for any row where the field's data type differs between old and new positions.

I'll scan the diff for the active file before making changes.

I will scan the diff for the following files:
- `solvas-am-biz-components\src\components\expression-builder\draggable-expression.tsx`

input.userRequirement: Review draggable-expression.tsx for the bug where dragging a date field row to a non-date position leaves stale ISO date string in value_ causing Invalid date error

messages.body: Let me look at the relevant files to understand the data types context.

The bug is clear. When reordering, `value_` is carried over as-is from the old index. If the dragged row had a date value (ISO string), it gets placed at the new index where the field has a different data type. The `FormCustomDatePicker` no longer renders there, but the stale ISO string in the Formik field causes the date picker's validation to fire (since the field name is reused), showing "Invalid date".

The fix: when the field at the old index had data type `DATE` but the field at the new index (after reorder) does not — or vice versa — clear `value_` and `value2_`. We need access to `ddxData` to look up field data types.

input.explanation: Fix the drag-drop bug by clearing value_ and value2_ when the field data type changes after reorder, preventing stale ISO date strings from causing Invalid date errors in non-date fields.

messages.body: No it should not be like this. The values should persist

You're right. The values should persist after reorder. The real issue is that the `FormCustomDatePicker` has stale Formik error state on the field name (e.g. `value_1`) from when it was a date field. When the field becomes non-date after reorder, the date picker unmounts but its error remains in Formik's state.

The fix should be in `FormCustomDatePicker` — clear the error on unmount so it doesn't bleed into whatever component next uses that field name.

diffs.oldStr: const [field, meta, helpers] = useField<string | null>({

input.explanation: Clear Formik error on unmount so stale date validation errors don't persist when the component is replaced by a non-date field input.

messages.body: Wait, that edit duplicated the `useField` declaration. Let me fix that:
There's a duplicate `useField` and duplicate `useEffect` cleanup. Also the first `useEffect` references `helpers` before it's declared. Let me fix this properly:

input.explanation: Remove the duplicate useField declaration and the misplaced useEffect that referenced helpers before it was declared.

messages.body: Also revert the `draggable-expression.tsx` changes from the previous incorrect fix:

input.explanation: Revert draggable-expression.tsx to its original state since the fix belongs in FormCustomDatePicker.

messages.body: do we need to cleanup other states as well? There is still the issue
Let me look at what other state the date picker sets that could persist — touched and value are the likely culprits too.

The real problem is clearer now. The `errorState` is `meta.touched && Boolean(meta.error)`. On unmount we clear the error, but `touched` remains `true` in Formik. When the new non-date component mounts on the same field name, `meta.touched` is still `true`. Then the `validate` function on the date picker fires again during Formik's revalidation cycle (because `enableReinitialize` triggers it), sees the ISO date string that was carried over in `value_`, and sets the error again — even on the newly mounted component.

The fix needs to clear both `touched` and `error` on unmount:

input.explanation: Also reset touched on unmount so stale touched state doesn't cause the error to re-appear on the next component using the same field name.

messages.body:
But there's still the `validate` function issue — the date picker registers a `validate` function via `useField` that runs on every Formik validation cycle. Even after unmount, if Formik re-validates (which `enableReinitialize` triggers), the validate function from the old field registration may still fire against the ISO date value.

The real fix is that the `validate` function should not be registered on the field at all when the component unmounts. Formik unregisters the field's validate when the component unmounts, but `enableReinitialize` causes `setValues` which triggers validation — and the new `FormTextField` at that field name has no validate, so it won't show an error. The issue is the **timing**: the `setErrorFormat` uses `setTimeout(..., 0)` which fires *after* the reorder's `setValues`, meaning the error gets set after the new component has already mounted.

That `setTimeout` in `setErrorFormat` is the culprit — it escapes the current render cycle and sets the error after the reorder has completed. We need to cancel it on unmount:

input.explanation: Track the setTimeout ref so it can be cancelled on unmount, preventing the async error from firing after the component is gone.

messages.body: Now wire `errorTimeoutRef` into both places that use `setTimeout`:

input.explanation: Replace both raw setTimeout calls with tracked ones via errorTimeoutRef so they can be cancelled on unmount.

messages.body:
Task: I have to use classic url for reporting service in modeling-ui repository, which will be through this iFrame.
1. Explain how iframe works?
2. How can I use this in other UI repo to navigate to reporting service?

example url for one of the tenant
https://am-wilmington-biz.solvasambiz.alterdomusdna.com/Apps/Reports/RunReport/ViewReport.asp?method=SaveReport&rs_path=Hrithik.Kumar@solvassolutions.com_7_59_40_PM
Let me look at the relevant files to understand the existing iframe setup better.

Here's a clear breakdown of both questions:

---

## 1. How iframe works (in this codebase)

The iframe acts as an embedded browser window inside the React app that loads the classic ASP-based UI. Here's the flow:

```
React App (modeling-ui)
    └── IFrameContainer
            ├── Fetches classic base URL → GET /solvasam-bff/v1/classicurl
            │       └── Returns: https://am-wilmington-biz.solvasambiz.alterdomusdna.com
            ├── Appends the route path → /Apps/Reports/RunReport/ViewReport.asp?...
            ├── Appends context params (portfolio ID, etc.)
            └── Sets iframe.src = full resolved URL
```

Key pieces:
- `getTargetClassicUrlBase` — fetches/caches the tenant's base URL from BFF
- `loadHtmlIntoIframe` — builds the full URL and sets `iframe.src`
- `appendContextParamsToUrl` — injects context (e.g. `parm` for portfolio) into the URL
- `listenForIFrameMessage` — listens for `postMessage` events from the classic app (page loads, modal returns, etc.)

---

## 2. How to use this for the Reporting Service in modeling-ui

The reporting URL you have is a **full absolute URL**, not a relative path. The existing `IFrameContainer` fetches the base URL from BFF and prepends it to a relative `targetClassicUrl`. For reporting, you have two options:

**Option A — Use `targetClassicUrlOverride` via hash (already supported)**

Navigate to the iframe route and pass the full URL as an override in the hash:

```
#/reporting?targetClassicUrlOverride=https%3A%2F%2Fam-wilmington-biz.solvasambiz.alterdomusdna.com%2FApps%2FReports%2FRunReport%2FViewReport.asp%3Fmethod%3DSaveReport%26rs_path%3DHrithik.Kumar%40solvassolutions.com_7_59_40_PM
```

**Option B — Pass the relative path as `targetClassicUrl` (recommended)**

In your `Routes.tsx` in modeling-ui, register the reporting route like this:

```tsx
<Route
  path="/reporting"
  element={
    <IFrameContainer
      navigationId="reporting"
      targetClassicUrl="/Apps/Reports/RunReport/ViewReport.asp"
      targetReactUrl="/reporting"
    />
  }
/>
```

The base URL (`https://am-wilmington-biz.solvasambiz.alterdomusdna.com`) is fetched automatically per tenant from the BFF — so you only pass the path. The `method` and `rs_path` query params can be appended dynamically:

```tsx
targetClassicUrl="/Apps/Reports/RunReport/ViewReport.asp?method=SaveReport&rs_path=Hrithik.Kumar@solvassolutions.com_7_59_40_PM"
```

Option B is cleaner because the tenant base URL is resolved at runtime via `getTargetClassicUrlBase`, making it work across all tenants (wilmington, etc.) without hardcoding the domain.
